message.content: J'aimerais transformer ce repository plutôt que d'avoir quelque chose qui est unitaire ici spécifique au multi-anvre AWS. J'aimerais en fait créer un repository qui est véritablement une bibliothèque d'exemples Terraform pour pouvoir taper dedans et avoir un seul répertoire de travail avec un ensemble de sous-projets typiquement, multi-anvre AWS, qui soit dans un sous-répertoire avec son REITME dédié, l'outillage dédié. Propose-moi avant toute chose une structure cohérente qui vient des bonnes pratiques pour ce type de projet, on va dire exemple. Déplacer les éléments qui sont à déplacer et propose-moi un plan complet pour créer cette structure qui sera prête à recevoir d'autres exemples. Typiquement, le prochain exemple que je vais ajouter et qui fera l'objet d'un live technique, c'est comment utiliser les versions un peu release candidates de Terraform pour préparer l'arrivée d'une feature qui permettra de simplifier l'écriture de certaines variables.

addedBlocks:
## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

attachment.content:
- handoff: End-of-session handoff. Reconcile the project's tracking file (mark done resolved, promote every leftover to a live item with value+trigger, log decisions/debt/lessons), verify green via the project's oracle command, confirm nothing lives only in chat or a disposable note, persist a disposable resume prompt (with a cold-start reload list of the links that mattered this session) into the tracking file, checkpoint-commit, then compact the note. Project-agnostic. Use when wrapping up, pausing, "prep handoff", "close the session", or "generate a resume prompt".
- linkedin-review: Multi-agent parallel review of a LinkedIn profile against benchmarks, via Playwright MCP browser navigation. Spawns N specialized personas (decideur-client, commercial-esn, personal-branding, concurrent-freelance, copywriter, ats-keywords, seo-linkedin) in parallel, each producing an independent critique. Returns a synthesized report with convergences, conflicts, prioritized actions. Use when the user asks to audit/review their LinkedIn profile.
- review-plan: Multi-agent parallel review of a plan, design doc, or architecture proposal. Spawns N expert personas (product, security, performance, seo, devex, cloud-architecture, finops, compliance, accessibility) in parallel, each producing an independent critique. Returns a synthesized report with convergences, conflicts, and decisions to make. Use when the user asks to review a plan, audit a design, get multiple perspectives on a doc, or run a "multi-agent review".
- rtk: RTK (Rust Token Killer) proxy meta-commands. Use when the user asks for RTK token-savings analytics, command history, missed-opportunity discovery, or wants to bypass the auto-rewrite hook (rtk proxy). The PreToolUse hook auto-rewrites Bash commands transparently - do not invoke this skill for normal commands.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

===levels.json===
[
  { "name": "level-0",      "dir": "level-0-single-env" },
  { "name": "level-1",      "dir": "level-1-workspaces" },
  { "name": "level-2/dev",  "dir": "level-2-root-per-env/dev" },
  { "name": "level-2/prod", "dir": "level-2-root-per-env/prod" },
  { "name": "level-3",      "dir": "level-3-specialization" }
]

===terraform.yml===
name: Terraform quality

on:
  push:
    branches: ['**']
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

concurrency:
  group: terraform-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

permissions:
  contents: read

jobs:
  setup:
    name: Load levels matrix
    # Always run on push; on pull_request, run only for fork PRs
    # (same-repo PRs are already covered by the push event).
    if: github.event_name == 'push' || github.event.pull_request.head.repo.full_name != github.repository
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      levels: ${{ steps.load.outputs.levels }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd # v6.0.2
      - id: load
        run: echo "levels=$(jq -c . .github/levels.json)" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

  fmt:
    name: terraform fmt
    needs: setup
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd # v6.0.2
      - uses: ./.github/actions/tf-setup
        with:
          install-tflint: 'false'
      - run: terraform fmt -check -recursive -diff

  quality:
    name: validate + lint + scan (${{ matrix.cell.name }})
    needs: setup
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        cell: ${{ fromJSON(needs.setup.outputs.levels) }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd # v6.0.2

      - uses: ./.github/actions/tf-setup

      - name: terraform init (no backend)
        working-directory: ${{ matrix.cell.dir }}
        run: terraform init -backend=false -input=false

      - name: terraform validate
        working-directory: ${{ matrix.cell.dir }}
        run: terraform validate -no-color

      - name: tflint init
        working-directory: ${{ matrix.cell.dir }}
        run: tflint --init
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: tflint
        working-directory: ${{ matrix.cell.dir }}
        run: tflint --recursive --format compact

      - name: trivy config scan
        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@57a97c7e7821a5776cebc9bb87c984fa69cba8f1 # v0.35.0
        with:
          scan-type: config
          scan-ref: ${{ matrix.cell.dir }}
          exit-code: '1'
          ignore-unfixed: true
          severity: HIGH,CRITICAL
===release.yml===
name: Release

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  workflow_dispatch:

concurrency:
  group: release-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: false

permissions:
  contents: write
  pull-requests: write

jobs:
  release-please:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: googleapis/release-please-action@5c625bfb5d1ff62eadeeb3772007f7f66fdcf071 # v4.4.1
        with:
          config-file: .github/release-please-config.json
          manifest-file: .github/.release-please-manifest.json

content.content:
===README===
# Terraform Multi-Environment Demo (AWS)

[![Terraform quality](https://github.com/bgauduch/demo-terraform-multi-env-aws/actions/workflows/terraform.yml/badge.svg?branch=main)](https://github.com/bgauduch/demo-terraform-multi-env-aws/actions/workflows/terraform.yml)
[![Release](https://github.com/bgauduch/demo-terraform-multi-env-aws/actions/workflows/release.yml/badge.svg?branch=main)](https://github.com/bgauduch/demo-terraform-multi-env-aws/actions/workflows/release.yml)
[![Latest release](https://img.shields.io/github/v/release/bgauduch/demo-terraform-multi-env-aws?sort=semver&display_name=tag)](https://github.com/bgauduch/demo-terraform-multi-env-aws/releases/latest)

Progressive demonstration of multi-environment management with Terraform CE (Community Edition) on AWS.

Supporting content for the "Matinale Tech" Twitch live stream.

> **⚠️ Demo only - not for production use**
>
> This repository is intended as a pedagogical demo to illustrate multi-environment patterns. Configuration is deliberately kept minimal to keep the focus on multi-env structure.
>
> For production use, you should at minimum:
> - use a **remote backend** (S3, or HCP Terraform) with locking instead of the local state used here
> - pin provider and module versions strictly, and manage credentials via a proper secrets mechanism
> - wrap everything in a CI/CD pipeline with policy-as-code, plan reviews, and drift detection

> **Note**: these demos and approaches concern Terraform CE. In CE mode, you have to build and maintain all the CI/CD "glue" yourself (S3 backend, DynamoDB locking, pipelines, credentials management, policy as code, etc.). For an out-of-the-box experience, head to [HCP Terraform](https://www.hashicorp.com/products/terraform/pricing/) - the cost of licenses largely covers the build and run costs of a custom CI/CD stack around TF CE.

## Prerequisites

- Terraform >= 1.5.0
- AWS CLI configured with valid credentials
- Default region: `eu-west-1` (Paris)

## Deployed resources

Simple network topology (free, quick to create/destroy):

- **VPC** with a CIDR variable per environment
- **2 subnets**: public + private (computed via `cidrsubnet()`)
- **Tags**: Project, Environment, ManagedBy

## Structure

Each level is self-contained and can be run independently.

```
level-0-single-env/         # One root module, a single environment
level-1-workspaces/         # One root module, Terraform workspaces
level-2-root-per-env/       # One root module per env + shared modules
level-3-specialization/     # One root module + specialization via -backend-config and -var-file
```

## Level 0 - Single Environment

"Monolithic" approach: one root module, one state, one environment.

```bash
cd level-0-single-env
terraform init
terraform plan
terraform apply
terraform destroy
```

- (+) simple, quick to set up
- (-) impossible to manage multiple environments without duplicating code

## Level 1 - Workspaces

A single root module using `terraform.workspace` to differentiate environments. The state is automatically separated per workspace.

```bash
cd level-1-workspaces
terraform init

terraform workspace new dev
terraform plan
terraform apply

terraform workspace new prod
terraform plan
terraform apply

# Cleanup
terraform workspace select dev && terraform destroy
terraform workspace select prod && terraform destroy
```

- (+) state automatically separated per workspace, zero code duplication
- (-) env config hardcoded in locals, risk of applying on the wrong workspace, no real isolation
- Approach discouraged by the [official docs](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/cli/workspaces#when-not-to-use-multiple-workspaces) for multi-env management

## Level 2 - Root Module per Environment

Structure recommended by the [official Terraform docs](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/style) for users outside HCP Terraform/TFE.

One directory per env, each with its own state. Modules are shared.

```bash
# Dev
cd level-2-root-per-env/dev
terraform init
terraform plan
terraform apply

# Prod
cd ../prod
terraform init
terraform plan
terraform apply

# Cleanup
cd ../dev && terraform destroy
cd ../prod && terraform destroy
```

- (+) full state isolation, no risk of env mistake, reusable modules
- (-) duplication of root code (providers, outputs) between envs

## Level 3 - Root Module + Specialization

A single root module. The target environment is selected at execution time via:

- `terraform init -backend-config=env/<env>.backend.hcl` for the state
- `terraform plan/apply -var-file=env/<env>.tfvars` for the configuration

```bash
cd level-3-specialization

# Dev
terraform init -backend-config=env/dev.backend.hcl
terraform plan -var-file=env/dev.tfvars
terraform apply -var-file=env/dev.tfvars

# Prod (re-init required to change backend)
terraform init -reconfigure -backend-config=env/prod.backend.hcl
terraform plan -var-file=env/prod.tfvars
terraform apply -var-file=env/prod.tfvars

# Cleanup
terraform init -reconfigure -backend-config=env/dev.backend.hcl
terraform destroy -var-file=env/dev.tfvars
terraform init -reconfigure -backend-config=env/prod.backend.hcl
terraform destroy -var-file=env/prod.tfvars
```

- (+) zero code duplication, adding an env = 2 files (`.tfvars` + `.backend.hcl`)
- (-) requires an `init -reconfigure` to switch envs locally (transparent in CI/CD)

## Going further

- **Terragrunt**: wrapper around Terraform that pushes DRY even further (backend management, inputs, dependencies between modules). Caveat: wrapper-only mode, no easy way back once adopted - a structuring choice for the team.
- **Terraform Stacks**: recent HCP Terraform feature to orchestrate multiple root modules as a unit (deployments, deferred changes).

## Technical points demonstrated

- `cidrsubnet()` for dynamic CIDR computation
- `aws_availability_zones` data source for dynamic AZ selection
- `merge()` for tag composition
- `lookup()` with fallback (level 1)
- Backend / env variables separation (level 3)
- Provider-defined functions (v1.8+): `provider::aws::arn_parse()`, `provider::aws::arn_build()`

===tf-setup action===
name: Terraform toolchain
description: Install Terraform (version from .terraform-version) and TFLint, pinned via SHA.

inputs:
  install-tflint:
    description: Whether to install TFLint (set to 'false' to skip).
    required: false
    default: 'true'

runs:
  using: composite
  steps:
    - id: tfver
      shell: bash
      run: echo "version=$(tr -d '[:space:]' < "$GITHUB_WORKSPACE/.terraform-version")" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

    - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@5e8dbf3c6d9deaf4193ca7a8fb23f2ac83bb6c85 # v4.0.0
      with:
        terraform_version: ${{ steps.tfver.outputs.version }}
        terraform_wrapper: false

    - if: inputs.install-tflint == 'true'
      uses: terraform-linters/setup-tflint@b480b8fcdaa6f2c577f8e4fa799e89e756bb7c93 # v6.2.2
      with:
        tflint_version: latest

input.command: echo "===gitignore==="; cat .gitignore; echo "===tracked tfstate?==="; git ls-files | grep -E 'tfstate|\.terraform' || echo "none tracked"; echo "===tracked file count by top dir==="; git ls-files | awk -F/ '{print $1}' | sort | uniq -c

content.content:
===gitignore===
# Local .terraform directories
.terraform/

# .tfstate files
*.tfstate
*.tfstate.*

# Crash log files
crash.log
crash.*.log

# Exclude all .tfvars files, which are likely to contain sensitive data, such as
# password, private keys, and other secrets. These should not be part of version 
# control as they are data points which are potentially sensitive and subject 
# to change depending on the environment.
*.tfvars
*.tfvars.json

# Include env-specific tfvars (demo values, no secrets)
!**/env/*.tfvars

# Ignore override files as they are usually used to override resources locally and so
# are not checked in
override.tf
override.tf.json
*_override.tf
*_override.tf.json

# Ignore transient lock info files created by terraform apply
.terraform.tfstate.lock.info

# Include override files you do wish to add to version control using negated pattern
# !example_override.tf

# Include tfplan files to ignore the plan output of command: terraform plan -out=tfplan
# example: *tfplan*

# Ignore CLI configuration files
.terraformrc
terraform.rc

# AI coding agents - local state, settings, and caches
.claude/
.cursor/
.aider*
.continue/
.codeium/

# OS-generated files
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db

# Editor/IDE local config
.vscode/
.idea/
*.swp
*.swo

# Environment and secrets
.env
.env.*
!.env.example
===tracked tfstate?===
.terraform-version
level-0-single-env/.terraform.lock.hcl
level-1-workspaces/.terraform.lock.hcl
level-2-root-per-env/dev/.terraform.lock.hcl
level-2-root-per-env/prod/.terraform.lock.hcl
level-3-specialization/.terraform.lock.hcl

   1 .commitlintrc.yml
   2 .entire
   9 .github
   1 .gitignore
   1 .terraform-version
   1 .tflint.hcl
   1 AGENTS.md
   1 CHANGELOG.md
   1 CLAUDE.md
   5 level-0-single-env
   5 level-1-workspaces
  13 level-2-root-per-env
  13 level-3-specialization
   1 LICENSE
   1 README.md

aiTitle: Restructure Terraform examples repository with multi-workspace architecture

content.text:
Analyse terminée. Voici l'état actuel et ma proposition.

## Constat

- Tout le repo est dédié au démo multi-env AWS (4 `level-*/`).
- Tooling à la racine, couplé à ce seul exemple : `.terraform-version`, `.tflint.hcl`, `.github/levels.json` (matrice CI), `release-please` mono-package, `AGENTS.md` 100% AWS-multi-env.
- `tfstate` non trackés (gitignore OK), seuls les `.terraform.lock.hcl` sont versionnés. Le déplacement ne touche que des fichiers trackés → `git mv`.

## Structure proposée (bonnes pratiques "examples monorepo")

```
terraform-examples/                    # racine = catalogue, plus de TF à la racine
├── README.md                          # index/catalogue : 1 ligne + lien par exemple
├── AGENTS.md  (+ CLAUDE.md symlink)   # conventions repo + "comment ajouter un exemple"
├── LICENSE
├── CHANGELOG.md
├── .gitignore                         # repo-wide (inchangé)
├── .github/                           # CI partagée
│   ├── workflows/  actions/tf-setup/
│   └── ...
└── examples/
    ├── aws-multi-env/                 # ← contenu actuel déplacé ici
    │   ├── README.md                  # README actuel (détaillé)
    │   ├── AGENTS.md                  # specifics AWS-multi-env (extrait de l'actuel)
    │   ├── .terraform-version         # pin TF propre à l'exemple
    │   ├── .tflint.hcl                # lint propre à l'exemple
    │   ├── level-0-single-env/ … level-3-specialization/
    │   └── (matrice CI locale)
    └── terraform-rc-variables/        # ← prochain exemple, scaffold prêt
        ├── README.md                  # placeholder + objectif
        ├── .terraform-version         # version RC ici (ex. 1.x-rc)
        └── .tflint.hcl
```

Points clés derrière ce design :
- **Tooling par exemple** (`.terraform-version`, `.tflint.hcl`) : indispensable pour ton prochain exemple qui a besoin d'une **version RC de Terraform** différente du reste. C'est le déclencheur qui justifie de descendre le pin par exemple.
- **`examples/` parent** : racine propre, chaque exemple auto-portant avec son README/AGENTS/tooling.
- **`tf-setup` action** : ajouter un input pour lire le `.terraform-version` du dossier de l'exemple (au lieu de la racine), et supporter une version prerelease.
- **AGENTS.md** : la racine décrit la lib + le golden path "ajouter un exemple" ; le contenu AWS-multi-env actuel migre dans `examples/aws-multi-env/AGENTS.md`.

Quelques décisions structurantes avant que je détaille le plan d'exécution :

questions.question: Découverte des dossiers Terraform par la CI (matrice de validation) ?

options.label: Auto-discovery (recommandé)
options.description: Un step CI scanne examples/ et liste tout dossier racine TF (marqueur providers.tf). Ajouter un exemple = 0 edit CI. Remplace levels.json.

options.label: Manifeste par exemple
options.description: Chaque examples/<name>/ci.json liste ses dossiers TF ; la CI les agrège. Plus explicite, mais 1 fichier à maintenir par exemple.

options.label: Repo-level unique (recommandé)
options.description: Garde release-please mono-package : 1 version/CHANGELOG pour tout le repo. Simple, suffisant pour un repo pédagogique.

options.label: Par exemple (monorepo)
options.description: release-please multi-packages, tags avec composant (aws-multi-env-v1.2.0). Releases indépendantes, mais config plus lourde.

options.label: Garder le nom actuel pour l'instant
options.description: demo-terraform-multi-env-aws inchangé. Pas de casse de badges/URLs. On pourra renommer plus tard.

options.label: Renommer maintenant
options.description: Ex. terraform-examples / terraform-cookbook. Cohérent avec la nouvelle vocation, mais casse badges README + URLs existantes à mettre à jour.

questions.question: Nom du dossier de l'exemple actuel ?

options.label: aws-multi-env
options.description: Court, scope clair (cloud-pattern). Cohérent avec un futur aws-eks, gcp-..., terraform-rc-variables.

options.label: multi-env-aws

answers.Nouveau nom du repo GitHub ?: Alors, je compte mettre dedans des exemples réutilisables, des exemples pour illustrer des postes sur LinkedIn ou même des articles de blogs à venir et aussi des points de départ ou des sortes peut-être de TP pour des live tech, voilà, pour avoir une sorte de boiler, strike et puis passer en différentes steps dessus. Peut-être que ça implique de revoir un petit peu les règles de nommage des exemples, ou alors avoir plusieurs répertoires, tout simplement. Un répertoire paterne, un répertoire exemple générique, et puis un répertoire expérimentation. Après, ça impliquerait d'avoir à gérer le cycle de vie, bon, je sais pas trop. Peut-être qu'on peut spécialiser chacun des exemples directement dans l'heure et demie, tout bêtement, avec une taxonomie très simple que l'on indique clairement dans l'heure et demie à la racine.

content.text:
Je verrouille les choix et propose la taxonomie.

**Nom : `terraform-examples`** - le plus générique, couvre tous tes cas (réutilisable, illustration blog/LinkedIn, TP live-tech, expérimentation RC). `cookbook` connote trop "recettes", `lab` trop "expé seule".

**Taxonomie : pas de dossiers-catégories au top-level** (`patterns/`, `experiments/`... = dette de cycle de vie, un exemple peut changer de catégorie). À la place : `examples/` plat + un **`type` déclaré par exemple**, source de vérité = table catalogue dans le README racine. Zéro outillage, conforme à ton intuition.

Taxonomie minimale (1 `type` primaire + `tags` libres) :
- `pattern` — référence réutilisable, orientée prod (ex. `aws-multi-env`)
- `lab` — point de départ / TP live-tech à étapes progressives
- `experiment` — features preview/RC (ex. `terraform-rc-variables`)

---

## Plan d'exécution complet

### 1. Arborescence
```
terraform-examples/
├── README.md                 # catalogue : table [Example | Type | Tags | Desc | lien]
├── AGENTS.md (+CLAUDE.md →)   # conventions repo + golden path "ajouter un exemple"
├── LICENSE  CHANGELOG.md  .gitignore  .commitlintrc.yml
├── .github/                  # CI partagée (généralisée)
└── examples/
    ├── aws-multi-env/        # type: pattern
    │   ├── README.md  AGENTS.md  .terraform-version  .tflint.hcl
    │   └── level-0…level-3/
    └── terraform-rc-variables/   # type: experiment (scaffold)
        ├── README.md  .terraform-version  .tflint.hcl  main.tf(stub)
```

### 2. Déplacements (`git mv`, fichiers trackés uniquement)
- `level-0..3/` → `examples/aws-multi-env/`
- `.terraform-version`, `.tflint.hcl` → `examples/aws-multi-env/`
- `README.md` (détaillé) → `examples/aws-multi-env/README.md`
- Extraire la partie AWS-multi-env de `AGENTS.md` → `examples/aws-multi-env/AGENTS.md`
- `tfstate` locaux on-disk (gitignored) : `mv` au passage pour cohérence, sinon laissés.

### 3. Nouveaux fichiers racine
- **`README.md`** racine : pitch lib + table catalogue + section "Ajouter un exemple" + badges (URLs MAJ vers `terraform-examples`).
- **`AGENTS.md`** racine : réécrit en conventions repo (taxonomie, structure d'un exemple, tooling par exemple, golden path). `CLAUDE.md` reste un symlink.

### 4. CI — auto-discovery (remplace `levels.json`)
- `terraform.yml` : step `discover` qui `find examples -name providers.tf` → matrice JSON des dossiers racine TF. Ajouter un exemple = 0 edit CI.
- `tf-setup/action.yml` : nouvel input `working-directory` (défaut repo root) pour lire le `.terraform-version` de l'exemple ; gérer version prerelease (RC) via `setup-terraform`.
- `fmt` : reste `-recursive` à la racine (couvre tout `examples/`).
- Supprimer `.github/levels.json`.

### 5. Release / versioning — repo-level unique (inchangé)
- `release-please-config.json` : `package-name` → `terraform-examples`. Reste mono-package `"."`. Manifest inchangé (1.0.1).

### 6. Scaffold `terraform-rc-variables` (experiment)
- `README.md` (objectif : tester variables RC, étapes à venir), `.terraform-version` (pin RC), `.tflint.hcl`, `main.tf` stub minimal → la CI auto-discovery le valide dès l'ajout.

### 7. Validation avant commit
- `terraform fmt -check -recursive`
- `terraform validate` dans chaque dossier racine déplacé
- Vérifier que le `discover` CI liste bien les 5 cells + le stub.
- Commits conventionnels (probable `refactor!`/`feat!` : restructuration = breaking pour les URLs).

---

Points à trancher restant :
- **Renommage GitHub** (`gh repo rename terraform-examples`) : je le fais ou tu le fais côté UI ? (impacte remote, badges).
- Je rédige les nouveaux README/AGENTS en **anglais** (cohérent avec l'existant) ?

Si OK, je passe en exécution et j'écris d'abord `tasks/todo.md`. Tu valides ?

content.text:
Noté. Je précise le scaffold de cet exemple en conséquence.

**`examples/terraform-rc-variables/`** — `type: experiment`
- **Objectif** : tester une feature Terraform en RC qui simplifie l'écriture de certaines variables, sur un cas concret : **injecter dynamiquement une clé KMS dans un module via un ARN non statique et inconnu au `plan`** (valeur résolue à l'`apply`).
- **Tags** : `rc`, `variables`, `kms`, `unknown-at-plan`.
- **Contenu scaffold** : `README.md` (objectif + scénario + étapes à venir), `.terraform-version` (pin RC), `.tflint.hcl`, `main.tf` stub (module appelant + variable KMS ARN). La CI auto-discovery le valide dès l'ajout.
- **Note** : je vérifierai le **nom exact de la feature + version RC** contre la doc Terraform courante au moment de bâtir le stub (la valeur "inconnue au plan" pointe vraisemblablement vers les *ephemeral values* / *write-only arguments*, à confirmer pour ne pas écrire de doc fausse).

Cette précision ne change pas la structure globale — juste le contenu de l'exemple expérimental.

Deux micro-décisions pour débloquer l'exécution :

It illustrates four progressive patterns for managing multiple environments with Terraform CE on AWS. Configuration is deliberately minimal to keep focus on the multi-env structure itself.
8	
9	Key implication: do not introduce production hardening (remote backend, locking, CI/CD, secrets management, strict version pinning) unless the user explicitly asks. The README's "Demo only" callout lists what would be added for real use - treat that as out-of-scope by default.
10	
11	## Architecture: the four levels
12	
13	Each `level-*/` directory is an independent root module demonstrating one multi-env pattern. They are intentionally **not** factored into a shared codebase - the duplication between levels is the point, since it lets a reader compare approaches side by side.
14	
15	All levels deploy the same trivial topology (VPC + public subnet + private subnet, tagged `Project`/`Environment`/`ManagedBy`). The network code is the backdrop; the *structural* differences between levels are the lesson.
16	
17	- **level-0-single-env/**: flat root module, one state, one env. Baseline.
18	- **level-1-workspaces/**: single root module, env selected via `terraform.workspace`. Per-env config lives in a `locals` map resolved with `lookup()`. Demonstrates the approach the official docs discourage for multi-env.
19	- **level-2-root-per-env/**: `dev/` and `prod/` are separate root modules consuming a shared `modules/network/`. Full state isolation at the cost of root-code duplication.
20	- **level-3-specialization/**: single root module, env injected at runtime via `-backend-config=env/<env>.backend.hcl` and `-var-file=env/<env>.tfvars`. Switching env locally requires `terraform init -reconfigure`.
21	
22	When editing one level, keep changes scoped to that level unless the user explicitly asks to propagate a pattern. Cross-level consistency is *not* a goal - each level is a standalone teaching artifact.
23	
24	## Common commands
25	
26	Always `cd` into the relevant `level-*/` directory first. The README has per-level command sequences; the canonical ones:
27	
28	```bash
29	# Levels 0, 1, 2 (standard)
30	terraform init
31	terraform plan
32	terraform apply
33	terraform destroy
34	
35	# Level 1 workspaces
36	terraform workspace new <env> && terraform workspace select <env>
37	
38	# Level 3 specialization (env passed at CLI)
39	terraform init -backend-config=env/<env>.backend.hcl
40	terraform plan  -var-file=env/<env>.tfvars
41	terraform apply -var-file=env/<env>.tfvars
42	# switching env locally:
43	terraform init -reconfigure -backend-config=env/<other>.backend.hcl
44	```
45	
46	Validation before committing: `terraform fmt -recursive` and `terraform validate` (run inside each level directory for `validate`).
47	
48	## Prerequisites
49	
50	- Terraform >= 1.5.0
51	- AWS CLI with valid credentials
52	- Default region: `eu-west-1`
53	
54	## Conventions in this repo
55	
56	- Provider-defined functions from AWS provider v1.8+ are intentionally showcased (`provider::aws::arn_parse`, `provider::aws::arn_build`); keep them when touching surrounding code.
57	- Subnet CIDRs are always computed via `cidrsubnet(vpc_cidr, 8, N)` rather than hardcoded - preserve this pattern when extending.
58	- Tags are composed with `merge(local.common_tags, { Name = ... })` - follow the same shape for new resources.
59	
60	## Repository standards
61	
62	- **Git flow**: GitHub flow (short-lived feature branches off `main`, PR review, squash-merge).
63	- **Commit messages**: [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) - `<type>(<scope>): <subject>`.
64	  - Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `docs`, `chore`, `refactor`, `test`, `ci`, `build`.
65	  - Breaking changes: append `!` after type/scope (e.g. `feat(network)!: ...`) or add a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer.
66	  - Keep subject imperative, lowercase, no trailing period, ~72 chars max.
67	- **Versioning**: [SemVer](https://semver.org/) - `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`.
68	- **Validation before commit**: run `terraform fmt -recursive` and `terraform validate` (inside each touched level directory) before opening a PR.

file.content:
# AGENTS.md

This file provides guidance to AI coding agents (Claude Code, Cursor, OpenAI Codex, Aider, etc.) when working with code in this repository. A `CLAUDE.md` symlink points here so Claude Code picks it up automatically.

## Purpose and scope

This is a **pedagogical demo** (supporting content for the "Matinale Tech" Twitch live stream), not production code. It illustrates four progressive patterns for managing multiple environments with Terraform CE on AWS. Configuration is deliberately minimal to keep focus on the multi-env structure itself.

Key implication: do not introduce production hardening (remote backend, locking, CI/CD, secrets management, strict version pinning) unless the user explicitly asks. The README's "Demo only" callout lists what would be added for real use - treat that as out-of-scope by default.

## Architecture: the four levels

Each `level-*/` directory is an independent root module demonstrating one multi-env pattern. They are intentionally **not** factored into a shared codebase - the duplication between levels is the point, since it lets a reader compare approaches side by side.

All levels deploy the same trivial topology (VPC + public subnet + private subnet, tagged `Project`/`Environment`/`ManagedBy`). The network code is the backdrop; the *structural* differences between levels are the lesson.

- **level-0-single-env/**: flat root module, one state, one env. Baseline.
- **level-1-workspaces/**: single root module, env selected via `terraform.workspace`. Per-env config lives in a `locals` map resolved with `lookup()`. Demonstrates the approach the official docs discourage for multi-env.
- **level-2-root-per-env/**: `dev/` and `prod/` are separate root modules consuming a shared `modules/network/`. Full state isolation at the cost of root-code duplication.
- **level-3-specialization/**: single root module, env injected at runtime via `-backend-config=env/<env>.backend.hcl` and `-var-file=env/<env>.tfvars`. Switching env locally requires `terraform init -reconfigure`.

When editing one level, keep changes scoped to that level unless the user explicitly asks to propagate a pattern. Cross-level consistency is *not* a goal - each level is a standalone teaching artifact.

## Common commands

Always `cd` into the relevant `level-*/` directory first. The README has per-level command sequences; the canonical ones:

```bash
# Levels 0, 1, 2 (standard)
terraform init
terraform plan
terraform apply
terraform destroy

# Level 1 workspaces
terraform workspace new <env> && terraform workspace select <env>

# Level 3 specialization (env passed at CLI)
terraform init -backend-config=env/<env>.backend.hcl
terraform plan  -var-file=env/<env>.tfvars
terraform apply -var-file=env/<env>.tfvars
# switching env locally:
terraform init -reconfigure -backend-config=env/<other>.backend.hcl
```

Validation before committing: `terraform fmt -recursive` and `terraform validate` (run inside each level directory for `validate`).

## Prerequisites

- Terraform >= 1.5.0
- AWS CLI with valid credentials
- Default region: `eu-west-1`

## Conventions in this repo

- Provider-defined functions from AWS provider v1.8+ are intentionally showcased (`provider::aws::arn_parse`, `provider::aws::arn_build`); keep them when touching surrounding code.
- Subnet CIDRs are always computed via `cidrsubnet(vpc_cidr, 8, N)` rather than hardcoded - preserve this pattern when extending.
- Tags are composed with `merge(local.common_tags, { Name = ... })` - follow the same shape for new resources.

## Repository standards

- **Git flow**: GitHub flow (short-lived feature branches off `main`, PR review, squash-merge).
- **Commit messages**: [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) - `<type>(<scope>): <subject>`.
  - Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `docs`, `chore`, `refactor`, `test`, `ci`, `build`.
  - Breaking changes: append `!` after type/scope (e.g. `feat(network)!: ...`) or add a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer.
  - Keep subject imperative, lowercase, no trailing period, ~72 chars max.
- **Versioning**: [SemVer](https://semver.org/) - `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`.
- **Validation before commit**: run `terraform fmt -recursive` and `terraform validate` (inside each touched level directory) before opening a PR.

## Purpose and scope

All levels deploy the same trivial topology (VPC + public subnet + private subnet, tagged `Project`/`Environment`/`ManagedBy`). The network code is the backdrop; the *structural* differences between levels are the lesson.

- **level-0-single-env/**: flat root module, one state, one env. Baseline.
- **level-1-workspaces/**: single root module, env selected via `terraform.workspace`. Per-env config lives in a `locals` map resolved with `lookup()`. Demonstrates the approach the official docs discourage for multi-env.
- **level-2-root-per-env/**: `dev/` and `prod/` are separate root modules consuming a shared `modules/network/`. Full state isolation at the cost of root-code duplication.
- **level-3-specialization/**: single root module, env injected at runtime via `-backend-config=env/<env>.backend.hcl` and `-var-file=env/<env>.tfvars`. Switching env locally requires `terraform init -reconfigure`.

When editing one level, keep changes scoped to that level unless the user explicitly asks to propagate a pattern. Cross-level consistency is *not* a goal - each level is a standalone teaching artifact.

## Common commands

Always `cd` into the relevant `level-*/` directory first. The README has per-level command sequences; the canonical ones:

```bash
# Levels 0, 1, 2 (standard)
terraform init
terraform plan
terraform apply
terraform destroy

# Level 1 workspaces

# Level 3 specialization (env passed at CLI)

terraform plan  -var-file=env/<env>.tfvars
terraform apply -var-file=env/<env>.tfvars
# switching env locally:
terraform init -reconfigure -backend-config=env/<other>.backend.hcl
```

Validation before committing: `terraform fmt -recursive` and `terraform validate` (run inside each level directory for `validate`).

## Prerequisites

- Terraform >= 1.5.0 (pinned via this example's `.terraform-version`)
- AWS CLI with valid credentials
- Default region: `eu-west-1`

## Conventions in this example

- Provider-defined functions from AWS provider v1.8+ are intentionally showcased (`provider::aws::arn_parse`, `provider::aws::arn_build`); keep them when touching surrounding code.
- Subnet CIDRs are always computed via `cidrsubnet(vpc_cidr, 8, N)` rather than hardcoded - preserve this pattern when extending.
- Tags are composed with `merge(local.common_tags, { Name = ... })` - follow the same shape for new resources.

input.content:
# AGENTS.md

Guidance for AI coding agents (Claude Code, Cursor, OpenAI Codex, Aider, etc.) working in this repository. A `CLAUDE.md` symlink points here so Claude Code picks it up automatically. Each example also has its own nested `AGENTS.md` with example-specific guidance - read it before editing inside an example.

## What this repo is

A **library of self-contained Terraform examples**. Each lives under `examples/<name>/` and is independent: its own `README.md`, `AGENTS.md`, tooling (`.terraform-version`, `.tflint.hcl`) and Terraform root module(s). Examples are **not** factored into shared code - isolation is intentional so each can be read, run, and live-demoed on its own.

Use cases served: reusable patterns, illustrations for blog/LinkedIn posts, and starting points / labs for live tech sessions.

## Taxonomy

Every example declares a primary `type` plus free-form tags. Source of truth is the catalogue table in the root `README.md`; each example's `README.md`/`AGENTS.md` restates its type.

- `pattern` - reusable, production-leaning reference.
- `lab` - starting point / TP with progressive steps for a live session.
- `experiment` - preview/RC features, may be intentionally unstable.

Do **not** create top-level category directories (`patterns/`, `experiments/`...). Keep `examples/` flat and classify via `type` + tags. This avoids lifecycle churn when an example changes category.

## How to add an example (golden path)

1. `mkdir examples/<name>/` - kebab-case, scope-first (`aws-multi-env`, `terraform-rc-variables`).
2. Add `README.md` (state its `type` + tags, objective, run steps) and `AGENTS.md` (example-specific guidance).
3. Add per-example tooling: `.terraform-version` (pin, may be an RC/prerelease for `experiment` types) and `.tflint.hcl`.
4. Add the Terraform root module(s). Any directory containing a `providers.tf` is auto-discovered by CI - no CI edit needed to validate it.
5. Register a row in the root `README.md` catalogue table.

## CI

# AGENTS.md

Guidance for AI coding agents (Claude Code, Cursor, OpenAI Codex, Aider, etc.) working in this repository. A `CLAUDE.md` symlink points here so Claude Code picks it up automatically. Each example also has its own nested `AGENTS.md` with example-specific guidance - read it before editing inside an example.

## What this repo is

A **library of self-contained Terraform examples**. Each lives under `examples/<name>/` and is independent: its own `README.md`, `AGENTS.md`, tooling (`.terraform-version`, `.tflint.hcl`) and Terraform root module(s). Examples are **not** factored into shared code - isolation is intentional so each can be read, run, and live-demoed on its own.

Use cases served: reusable patterns, illustrations for blog/LinkedIn posts, and starting points / labs for live tech sessions.

## Taxonomy

Every example declares a primary `type` plus free-form tags. Source of truth is the catalogue table in the root `README.md`; each example's `README.md`/`AGENTS.md` restates its type.

- `pattern` - reusable, production-leaning reference.
- `lab` - starting point / TP with progressive steps for a live session.
- `experiment` - preview/RC features, may be intentionally unstable.

Do **not** create top-level category directories (`patterns/`, `experiments/`...). Keep `examples/` flat and classify via `type` + tags. This avoids lifecycle churn when an example changes category.

## How to add an example (golden path)

1. `mkdir examples/<name>/` - kebab-case, scope-first (`aws-multi-env`, `terraform-rc-variables`).
2. Add `README.md` (state its `type` + tags, objective, run steps) and `AGENTS.md` (example-specific guidance).
3. Add per-example tooling: `.terraform-version` (pin, may be an RC/prerelease for `experiment` types) and `.tflint.hcl`.
4. Add the Terraform root module(s). Any directory containing a `providers.tf` is auto-discovered by CI - no CI edit needed to validate it.
5. Register a row in the root `README.md` catalogue table.

## CI

`.github/workflows/terraform.yml` auto-discovers Terraform root modules (directories containing `providers.tf`) under `examples/` and runs `validate` + `tflint` + `trivy` per module in a matrix. `terraform fmt -check -recursive` runs once at the root. The `tf-setup` composite action installs Terraform from the target example's `.terraform-version`. Adding an example requires no CI change.

## Repository standards

- **Git flow**: GitHub flow (short-lived feature branches off `main`, PR review, squash-merge).
- **Commit messages**: [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) - `<type>(<scope>): <subject>`. Use the example name as scope when relevant (e.g. `feat(aws-multi-env): ...`).
  - Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `docs`, `chore`, `refactor`, `test`, `ci`, `build`.
  - Breaking changes: append `!` after type/scope or add a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer.
  - Keep subject imperative, lowercase, no trailing period, ~72 chars max.
- **Versioning**: [SemVer](https://semver.org/), repo-level single release line via release-please.
- **Validation before commit**: `terraform fmt -recursive` (root) and `terraform validate` inside each touched root module.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# AGENTS.md

This file provides guidance to AI coding agents (Claude Code, Cursor, OpenAI Codex, Aider, etc.) when working with code in this repository. A `CLAUDE.md` symlink points here so Claude Code picks it up automatically.

## Purpose and scope

This is a **pedagogical demo** (supporting content for the "Matinale Tech" Twitch live stream), not production code. It illustrates four progressive patterns for managing multiple environments with Terraform CE on AWS. Configuration is deliberately minimal to keep focus on the multi-env structure itself.

Key implication: do not introduce production hardening (remote backend, locking, CI/CD, secrets management, strict version pinning) unless the user explicitly asks. The README's "Demo only" callout lists what would be added for real use - treat that as out-of-scope by default.

## Architecture: the four levels

Each `level-*/` directory is an independent root module demonstrating one multi-env pattern. They are intentionally **not** factored into a shared codebase - the duplication between levels is the point, since it lets a reader compare approaches side by side.

All levels deploy the same trivial topology (VPC + public subnet + private subnet, tagged `Project`/`Environment`/`ManagedBy`). The network code is the backdrop; the *structural* differences between levels are the lesson.

- **level-0-single-env/**: flat root module, one state, one env. Baseline.
- **level-1-workspaces/**: single root module, env selected via `terraform.workspace`. Per-env config lives in a `locals` map resolved with `lookup()`. Demonstrates the approach the official docs discourage for multi-env.
- **level-2-root-per-env/**: `dev/` and `prod/` are separate root modules consuming a shared `modules/network/`. Full state isolation at the cost of root-code duplication.
- **level-3-specialization/**: single root module, env injected at runtime via `-backend-config=env/<env>.backend.hcl` and `-var-file=env/<env>.tfvars`. Switching env locally requires `terraform init -reconfigure`.

When editing one level, keep changes scoped to that level unless the user explicitly asks to propagate a pattern. Cross-level consistency is *not* a goal - each level is a standalone teaching artifact.

## Common commands

Always `cd` into the relevant `level-*/` directory first. The README has per-level command sequences; the canonical ones:

```bash
# Levels 0, 1, 2 (standard)
terraform init
terraform plan
terraform apply
terraform destroy

# Level 1 workspaces
terraform workspace new <env> && terraform workspace select <env>

# Level 3 specialization (env passed at CLI)
terraform init -backend-config=env/<env>.backend.hcl
terraform plan  -var-file=env/<env>.tfvars
terraform apply -var-file=env/<env>.tfvars
# switching env locally:
terraform init -reconfigure -backend-config=env/<other>.backend.hcl
```

Validation before committing: `terraform fmt -recursive` and `terraform validate` (run inside each level directory for `validate`).

## Prerequisites

- Terraform >= 1.5.0
- AWS CLI with valid credentials
- Default region: `eu-west-1`

## Conventions in this repo

- Provider-defined functions from AWS provider v1.8+ are intentionally showcased (`provider::aws::arn_parse`, `provider::aws::arn_build`); keep them when touching surrounding code.
- Subnet CIDRs are always computed via `cidrsubnet(vpc_cidr, 8, N)` rather than hardcoded - preserve this pattern when extending.
- Tags are composed with `merge(local.common_tags, { Name = ... })` - follow the same shape for new resources.

## Repository standards

- **Git flow**: GitHub flow (short-lived feature branches off `main`, PR review, squash-merge).
- **Commit messages**: [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) - `<type>(<scope>): <subject>`.
  - Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `docs`, `chore`, `refactor`, `test`, `ci`, `build`.
  - Breaking changes: append `!` after type/scope (e.g. `feat(network)!: ...`) or add a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer.
  - Keep subject imperative, lowercase, no trailing period, ~72 chars max.
- **Versioning**: [SemVer](https://semver.org/) - `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`.
- **Validation before commit**: run `terraform fmt -recursive` and `terraform validate` (inside each touched level directory) before opening a PR.

# Terraform Examples

[![Terraform quality](https://github.com/bgauduch/terraform-examples/actions/workflows/terraform.yml/badge.svg?branch=main)](https://github.com/bgauduch/terraform-examples/actions/workflows/terraform.yml)
[![Release](https://github.com/bgauduch/terraform-examples/actions/workflows/release.yml/badge.svg?branch=main)](https://github.com/bgauduch/terraform-examples/actions/workflows/release.yml)
[![Latest release](https://img.shields.io/github/v/release/bgauduch/terraform-examples?sort=semver&display_name=tag)](https://github.com/bgauduch/terraform-examples/releases/latest)

A library of self-contained Terraform examples: reusable patterns, illustrations for blog/LinkedIn posts, and starting points for live tech sessions ("Matinale Tech").

Each example lives under [`examples/`](examples/) and is fully independent - its own `README.md`, tooling (`.terraform-version`, `.tflint.hcl`) and Terraform code. Pick one, `cd` into it, follow its README.

## Catalogue

| Example | Type | Tags | Description |
|---------|------|------|-------------|
| [`aws-multi-env`](examples/aws-multi-env/) | `pattern` | `aws` `multi-env` `backend` `workspaces` | Four progressive patterns for managing multiple environments with Terraform CE on AWS. |
| [`terraform-rc-variables`](examples/terraform-rc-variables/) | `experiment` | `rc` `deferred-actions` `kms` `unknown-at-plan` | Testing Terraform's experimental *deferred actions* (`plan -allow-deferral`) to inject a KMS key whose ARN is unknown at plan time. |

### Taxonomy

- `pattern` - reusable, production-leaning reference.
- `lab` - starting point / TP with progressive steps for a live session.
- `experiment` - preview/RC features, may be intentionally unstable.

## Adding an example

See the golden path in [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md). In short: create `examples/<name>/` with its own `README.md`, `.terraform-version`, `.tflint.hcl` and Terraform root module(s), then add a row to the catalogue above. CI auto-discovers any directory containing `providers.tf` - no CI change needed.

## Repository standards

- **Git flow**: GitHub flow (short-lived branches off `main`, PR review, squash-merge).
- **Commits**: [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/); use the example name as scope (`feat(aws-multi-env): ...`).
- **Versioning**: [SemVer](https://semver.org/), repo-level single release line via release-please.
- **Validation before commit**: `terraform fmt -recursive` (root) and `terraform validate` inside each touched root module.

content.text: Maintenant `tf-setup` : input `working-directory` + résolution du `.terraform-version` le plus proche (walk-up).

input.content:
name: Terraform toolchain
description: Install Terraform (version from the nearest .terraform-version) and TFLint, pinned via SHA.

inputs:
  working-directory:
    description: Directory to resolve the Terraform version from; walks up to the nearest .terraform-version (falls back to repo root).
    required: false
    default: '.'
  install-tflint:
    description: Whether to install TFLint (set to 'false' to skip).
    required: false
    default: 'true'

runs:
  using: composite
  steps:
    - id: tfver
      shell: bash
      working-directory: ${{ inputs.working-directory }}
      run: |
        # Walk up from the working dir to the repo root, picking the nearest .terraform-version.
        dir="$(pwd)"
        while [ "$dir" != "$GITHUB_WORKSPACE" ] && [ "$dir" != "/" ]; do
          [ -f "$dir/.terraform-version" ] && break
          dir="$(dirname "$dir")"
        done
        file="$dir/.terraform-version"
        [ -f "$file" ] || file="$GITHUB_WORKSPACE/.terraform-version"
        echo "version=$(tr -d '[:space:]' < "$file")" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

    - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@5e8dbf3c6d9deaf4193ca7a8fb23f2ac83bb6c85 # v4.0.0
      with:
        terraform_version: ${{ steps.tfver.outputs.version }}
        terraform_wrapper: false

    - if: inputs.install-tflint == 'true'
      uses: terraform-linters/setup-tflint@b480b8fcdaa6f2c577f8e4fa799e89e756bb7c93 # v6.2.2
      with:
        tflint_version: latest

  install-tflint:
    description: Whether to install TFLint (set to 'false' to skip).

  using: composite
  steps:
    - id: tfver
      shell: bash
      run: echo "version=$(tr -d '[:space:]' < "$GITHUB_WORKSPACE/.terraform-version")" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

    - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@5e8dbf3c6d9deaf4193ca7a8fb23f2ac83bb6c85 # v4.0.0
      with:
        terraform_version: ${{ steps.tfver.outputs.version }}
        terraform_wrapper: false

    - if: inputs.install-tflint == 'true'
      uses: terraform-linters/setup-tflint@b480b8fcdaa6f2c577f8e4fa799e89e756bb7c93 # v6.2.2

        tflint_version: latest

input.content:
name: Terraform toolchain
description: Install Terraform (version from the nearest .terraform-version) and TFLint, pinned via SHA.

inputs:
  working-directory:
    description: Directory to resolve the Terraform version from; walks up to the nearest .terraform-version (falls back to repo root).
    required: false
    default: '.'
  install-tflint:
    description: Whether to install TFLint (set to 'false' to skip).
    required: false
    default: 'true'

runs:
  using: composite
  steps:
    - id: tfver
      shell: bash
      working-directory: ${{ inputs.working-directory }}
      run: |
        # Walk up from the working dir to the repo root, picking the nearest .terraform-version.
        dir="$(pwd)"
        while [ "$dir" != "$GITHUB_WORKSPACE" ] && [ "$dir" != "/" ]; do
          [ -f "$dir/.terraform-version" ] && break
          dir="$(dirname "$dir")"
        done
        file="$dir/.terraform-version"
        [ -f "$file" ] || file="$GITHUB_WORKSPACE/.terraform-version"
        echo "version=$(tr -d '[:space:]' < "$file")" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

    - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@5e8dbf3c6d9deaf4193ca7a8fb23f2ac83bb6c85 # v4.0.0
      with:
        terraform_version: ${{ steps.tfver.outputs.version }}
        terraform_wrapper: false

    - if: inputs.install-tflint == 'true'
      uses: terraform-linters/setup-tflint@b480b8fcdaa6f2c577f8e4fa799e89e756bb7c93 # v6.2.2
      with:
        tflint_version: latest

file.content:
name: Terraform quality

on:
  push:
    branches: ['**']
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

concurrency:
  group: terraform-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

permissions:
  contents: read

jobs:
  setup:
    name: Load levels matrix
    # Always run on push; on pull_request, run only for fork PRs
    # (same-repo PRs are already covered by the push event).
    if: github.event_name == 'push' || github.event.pull_request.head.repo.full_name != github.repository
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      levels: ${{ steps.load.outputs.levels }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd # v6.0.2
      - id: load
        run: echo "levels=$(jq -c . .github/levels.json)" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

  fmt:
    name: terraform fmt
    needs: setup
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd # v6.0.2
      - uses: ./.github/actions/tf-setup
        with:
          install-tflint: 'false'
      - run: terraform fmt -check -recursive -diff

  quality:
    name: validate + lint + scan (${{ matrix.cell.name }})
    needs: setup
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        cell: ${{ fromJSON(needs.setup.outputs.levels) }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd # v6.0.2

      - uses: ./.github/actions/tf-setup

      - name: terraform init (no backend)
        working-directory: ${{ matrix.cell.dir }}
        run: terraform init -backend=false -input=false

      - name: terraform validate
        working-directory: ${{ matrix.cell.dir }}
        run: terraform validate -no-color

      - name: tflint init
        working-directory: ${{ matrix.cell.dir }}
        run: tflint --init
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: tflint
        working-directory: ${{ matrix.cell.dir }}
        run: tflint --recursive --format compact

      - name: trivy config scan
        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@57a97c7e7821a5776cebc9bb87c984fa69cba8f1 # v0.35.0
        with:
          scan-type: config
          scan-ref: ${{ matrix.cell.dir }}
          exit-code: '1'
          ignore-unfixed: true
          severity: HIGH,CRITICAL

input.content:
name: Terraform quality

on:
  push:
    branches: ['**']
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

concurrency:
  group: terraform-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

permissions:
  contents: read

jobs:
  discover:
    name: Discover Terraform root modules
    # Always run on push; on pull_request, run only for fork PRs
    # (same-repo PRs are already covered by the push event).
    if: github.event_name == 'push' || github.event.pull_request.head.repo.full_name != github.repository
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      cells: ${{ steps.find.outputs.cells }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd # v6.0.2
      - id: find
        # Any directory under examples/ containing providers.tf is a root module to validate.
        # name = path minus examples/ ; example = examples/<name> (holds the per-example tooling).
        run: |
          cells=$(find examples -name providers.tf -printf '%h\n' | sort \
            | jq -R -s '
                split("\n")
                | map(select(length > 0))
                | map({
                    dir: .,
                    name: (sub("^examples/"; "")),
                    example: (split("/")[0:2] | join("/"))
                  })')
          echo "cells=$(echo "$cells" | jq -c .)" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

  fmt:
    name: terraform fmt
    needs: discover
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd # v6.0.2
      - uses: ./.github/actions/tf-setup
        with:
          install-tflint: 'false'
      - run: terraform fmt -check -recursive -diff

  quality:
    name: validate + lint + scan (${{ matrix.cell.name }})
    needs: discover
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        cell: ${{ fromJSON(needs.discover.outputs.cells) }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd # v6.0.2

      - uses: ./.github/actions/tf-setup
        with:
          working-directory: ${{ matrix.cell.dir }}

      - name: terraform init (no backend)
        working-directory: ${{ matrix.cell.dir }}
        run: terraform init -backend=false -input=false

      - name: terraform validate
        working-directory: ${{ matrix.cell.dir }}
        run: terraform validate -no-color

      - name: tflint init
        working-directory: ${{ matrix.cell.dir }}
        run: tflint --init --config "${{ github.workspace }}/${{ matrix.cell.example }}/.tflint.hcl"
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: tflint
        working-directory: ${{ matrix.cell.dir }}
        run: tflint --recursive --format compact --config "${{ github.workspace }}/${{ matrix.cell.example }}/.tflint.hcl"

      - name: trivy config scan
        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@57a97c7e7821a5776cebc9bb87c984fa69cba8f1 # v0.35.0
        with:
          scan-type: config
          scan-ref: ${{ matrix.cell.dir }}
          exit-code: '1'
          ignore-unfixed: true
          severity: HIGH,CRITICAL

input.content:
# terraform-rc-variables

> **Type**: `experiment`  ·  **Tags**: `rc` `deferred-actions` `kms` `unknown-at-plan`
>
> Experimental and intentionally unstable - this example tracks a Terraform release-candidate feature and will be fleshed out live.

## Objective

Exercise Terraform's experimental **deferred actions** to simplify passing variables whose value is **not known at plan time**.

Concrete scenario: inject a **KMS key into a module via an ARN that is dynamic and not necessarily known during `plan`** (e.g. the key is created in the same apply). Today this forces awkward workarounds (two-phase applies, `-target`, placeholder values). Deferred actions let the plan tolerate the unknown value and defer the affected resources to apply.

## The feature

Deferred actions are enabled by passing `-allow-deferral` to `terraform plan`. They let `count` and `for_each` (in `module`, `resource`, and `data` blocks) carry unknown values and allow providers to react more flexibly to unknowns, instead of erroring out at plan time.

```bash
terraform init
terraform plan -allow-deferral -var 'kms_key_arn=<arn-or-unknown>'
```

## Status

Scaffold only. Before the live:

- [ ] Bump `.terraform-version` to the target Terraform RC that ships deferred actions.
- [ ] Wire the KMS key + consuming module so the ARN is genuinely unknown at plan.
- [ ] Demonstrate the plan succeeding with `-allow-deferral` vs failing without it.

The current files only surface the `kms_key_arn` input so the example validates and is picked up by CI.

content.text:
Compris — objectif mis à jour : je termine, je pousse une branche, et je ne reviens que quand la CI est verte. Je ne te dérange plus d'ici là.

Un risque que je veux écarter avant de pousser : ma CI charge désormais explicitement le `.tflint.hcl` de chaque exemple (`--config`), alors qu'avant il n'était jamais chargé (cwd = dossier `level`, sans `.tflint.hcl`). Le ruleset AWS pourrait donc révéler de nouveaux findings. Je vérifie `tflint` en local d'abord.

content.text: `tflint` absent. Je l'installe en local isolé (binaire dans un dossier temp, pas de pollution globale) pour pré-valider avant de pousser.

input.command:
set -e
TDIR="$(mktemp -d)"
cd "$TDIR"
# Detect arch
ARCH=$(uname -m); case "$ARCH" in arm64) A=arm64;; x86_64) A=amd64;; esac
VER=$(curl -s https://api.github.com/repos/terraform-linters/tflint/releases/latest | jq -r .tag_name)
echo "tflint $VER darwin_$A -> $TDIR"
curl -sL "https://github.com/terraform-linters/tflint/releases/download/${VER}/tflint_darwin_${A}.zip" -o tflint.zip
unzip -q tflint.zip
./tflint --version
echo "$TDIR" > /tmp/tflint_dir.txt

  --body "$(cat <<'MD'
## What

Transforms the repo from a single AWS multi-env demo into a **library of self-contained Terraform examples** under `examples/<name>/`.

Each example is independent: own `README.md`, `AGENTS.md`, tooling (`.terraform-version`, `.tflint.hcl`) and Terraform root module(s). Classified via a simple `type` (`pattern` | `lab` | `experiment`) + tags taxonomy, catalogued in the root README. No top-level category dirs (avoids lifecycle churn).

## Changes

- **Move**: multi-env demo → `examples/aws-multi-env/` (type `pattern`), via `git mv` (history preserved).
- **Scaffold**: `examples/terraform-rc-variables/` (type `experiment`) for the upcoming live on Terraform's experimental **deferred actions** (`plan -allow-deferral`) — scenario: inject a KMS key whose ARN is unknown at plan.
- **CI**: auto-discovers TF root modules (`providers.tf`) under `examples/`, resolves each example's `.terraform-version` (walk-up), loads each example's `.tflint.hcl`. Drops `.github/levels.json` — adding an example needs no CI change.
- **Docs**: root README = catalogue + golden path; root `AGENTS.md` = library conventions; per-example `AGENTS.md` for specifics.
- **Release**: release-please package renamed to `terraform-examples`.
- **Chore**: disable Entire telemetry.

## Validation (local)

- `terraform fmt -check -recursive` ✅
- `terraform validate` on all 6 root modules ✅
- `tflint` (with each example's config) on all 6 modules ✅

## Notes

- **BREAKING**: example paths moved under `examples/`; repo renamed to `terraform-examples` (GitHub keeps a redirect; badge/deep-link URLs updated). Done via `gh repo rename`.
- Carries `chore(gitignore): track entire tool config` (was on `chore/track-entire-config`, no separate PR).
MD
)" 2>&1 | tail -3

content.text:
Terminé. Livrable conforme : **branche poussée + PR ouverte + CI verte**.

## Résultat

- **Repo renommé** : `demo-terraform-multi-env-aws` → `terraform-examples` (remote local mis à jour, redirection GitHub conservée).
- **PR #10** (`refactor/terraform-examples-library` → `main`) : https://github.com/bgauduch/terraform-examples/pull/10
- **CI : 9/9 verts** — fmt, discover, 6 cellules `validate + lint + scan` (5 multi-env + rc), conventional commits. Aucun pending, aucun fail.

## Ce qui a changé

- **Structure** : `examples/` plat, taxonomie `type` (`pattern`|`lab`|`experiment`) + tags, source de vérité = table catalogue du README racine. Pas de dossiers-catégories (dette de cycle de vie évitée).
- **Déplacement** : démo multi-env → `examples/aws-multi-env/` via `git mv` (historique préservé), avec son `README`, `AGENTS.md`, `.terraform-version`, `.tflint.hcl`.
- **Scaffold** : `examples/terraform-rc-variables/` (experiment) ciblant les **deferred actions** (`plan -allow-deferral`) → injection KMS ARN inconnu au plan. Stub valide CI ; pin RC à poser au moment du live.
- **CI généralisée** : auto-découverte des roots via `providers.tf`, résolution du `.terraform-version` par exemple (walk-up), chargement du `.tflint.hcl` par exemple (`--config`). `levels.json` supprimé → **ajouter un exemple = 0 edit CI**.
- **release-please** : package → `terraform-examples`.
- **Chore isolé** : télémétrie Entire désactivée.

## À noter

- La branche embarque aussi `chore(gitignore): track entire tool config` (ff270ba, qui n'avait pas de PR). Si tu le veux dans une PR séparée, dis-le et je le sors par rebase.
- Suivis (hors PR) listés dans `tasks/todo.md` : câbler le stub rc à un vrai build RC le jour du live.

content.text:
Convention du repo : kebab-case, scope-first (`aws-multi-env`). Le tag `rc` est déjà porté par la taxonomie → inutile dans le nom du dossier. Propositions :

| Nom | Remarque |
|-----|----------|
| **`terraform-deferred-actions`** ✅ recommandé | Colle au nom exact de la feature, scope clair, cohérent avec `aws-multi-env`. |
| `deferred-actions-kms` | Inclut le scénario (KMS ARN inconnu au plan) mais mélange feature + cas d'usage. |
| `terraform-rc-deferred-actions` | Redondant avec le tag `rc` de la taxonomie. |

Je recommande **`terraform-deferred-actions`**.

Si tu valides, j'applique le renommage de bout en bout : `git mv` du dossier, MAJ du titre/`README` de l'exemple, de la ligne catalogue (`README` racine + tag `deferred-actions`), commit sur la branche, push — la CI auto-discovery se recale seule (aucun edit CI). Je le fais ?

message.content: Ok, go pour le rename sur le premier nom proposé. Une fois que tu auras fait ça, propose-moi dans un plan les éléments suivants. Il me faut en premier temps que cet exemple soit un root module qui crée une clé KMS et qui utilise un sous-module qui va lui déployer un bucket S3. En remontrer les variables de ce module, on va retrouver l'ARN de la clé KMS et donc l'objectif, c'est que le module soit capable de créer ou non, avec un count ou peu importe la méthodologie en interne, la ressource KMS pour l'assessier au bucket en fonction de la valeur de cette variable. S'il n'y a pas d'ARN qui est donné, il faut évidemment créer une clé pour chiffrer le bucket. Si il y a un ARN qui est donné, on va venir valider cet ARN avec une data source et l'utiliser pour le bucket, pour avoir un exemple le plus complet possible. Voilà, dans toute modification, propose-moi un plan détaillé que l'on viendra tracer dans les to-do.

# terraform-rc-variables

> **Type**: `experiment`  ·  **Tags**: `rc` `deferred-actions` `kms` `unknown-at-plan`
>
> Experimental and intentionally unstable - this example tracks a Terraform release-candidate feature and will be fleshed out live.

## Objective

Exercise Terraform's experimental **deferred actions** to simplify passing variables whose value is **not known at plan time**.

Concrete scenario: inject a **KMS key into a module via an ARN that is dynamic and not necessarily known during `plan`** (e.g. the key is created in the same apply). Today this forces awkward workarounds (two-phase applies, `-target`, placeholder values). Deferred actions let the plan tolerate the unknown value and defer the affected resources to apply.

## The feature

Deferred actions are enabled by passing `-allow-deferral` to `terraform plan`. They let `count` and `for_each` (in `module`, `resource`, and `data` blocks) carry unknown values and allow providers to react more flexibly to unknowns, instead of erroring out at plan time.

```bash
terraform init
terraform plan -allow-deferral -var 'kms_key_arn=<arn-or-unknown>'
```

## Status

Scaffold only. Before the live:

- [ ] Bump `.terraform-version` to the target Terraform RC that ships deferred actions.
- [ ] Wire the KMS key + consuming module so the ARN is genuinely unknown at plan.
- [ ] Demonstrate the plan succeeding with `-allow-deferral` vs failing without it.

The current files only surface the `kms_key_arn` input so the example validates and is picked up by CI.

# Terraform Examples

[![Terraform quality](https://github.com/bgauduch/terraform-examples/actions/workflows/terraform.yml/badge.svg?branch=main)](https://github.com/bgauduch/terraform-examples/actions/workflows/terraform.yml)
[![Release](https://github.com/bgauduch/terraform-examples/actions/workflows/release.yml/badge.svg?branch=main)](https://github.com/bgauduch/terraform-examples/actions/workflows/release.yml)
[![Latest release](https://img.shields.io/github/v/release/bgauduch/terraform-examples?sort=semver&display_name=tag)](https://github.com/bgauduch/terraform-examples/releases/latest)

A library of self-contained Terraform examples: reusable patterns, illustrations for blog/LinkedIn posts, and starting points for live tech sessions ("Matinale Tech").

Each example lives under [`examples/`](examples/) and is fully independent - its own `README.md`, tooling (`.terraform-version`, `.tflint.hcl`) and Terraform code. Pick one, `cd` into it, follow its README.

## Catalogue

| Example | Type | Tags | Description |
|---------|------|------|-------------|
| [`aws-multi-env`](examples/aws-multi-env/) | `pattern` | `aws` `multi-env` `backend` `workspaces` | Four progressive patterns for managing multiple environments with Terraform CE on AWS. |
| [`terraform-rc-variables`](examples/terraform-rc-variables/) | `experiment` | `rc` `deferred-actions` `kms` `unknown-at-plan` | Testing Terraform's experimental *deferred actions* (`plan -allow-deferral`) to inject a KMS key whose ARN is unknown at plan time. |

### Taxonomy

- `pattern` - reusable, production-leaning reference.
- `lab` - starting point / TP with progressive steps for a live session.
- `experiment` - preview/RC features, may be intentionally unstable.

## Adding an example

See the golden path in [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md). In short: create `examples/<name>/` with its own `README.md`, `.terraform-version`, `.tflint.hcl` and Terraform root module(s), then add a row to the catalogue above. CI auto-discovers any directory containing `providers.tf` - no CI change needed.

## Repository standards

- **Git flow**: GitHub flow (short-lived branches off `main`, PR review, squash-merge).
- **Commits**: [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/); use the example name as scope (`feat(aws-multi-env): ...`).
- **Versioning**: [SemVer](https://semver.org/), repo-level single release line via release-please.
- **Validation before commit**: `terraform fmt -recursive` (root) and `terraform validate` inside each touched root module.

lines: | [`aws-multi-env`](examples/aws-multi-env/) | `pattern` | `aws` `multi-env` `backend` `workspaces` | Four progressive patterns for managing multiple environments with Terraform CE on AWS. |

-| [`terraform-rc-variables`](examples/terraform-rc-variables/) | `experiment` | `rc` `deferred-actions` `kms` `unknown-at-plan` | Testing Terraform's experimental *deferred actions* (`plan -allow-deferral`) to inject a KMS key whose ARN is unknown at plan time. |

+| [`terraform-deferred-actions`](examples/terraform-deferred-actions/) | `experiment` | `rc` `deferred-actions` `kms` `unknown-at-plan` | Testing Terraform's experimental *deferred actions* (`plan -allow-deferral`) to inject a KMS key whose ARN is unknown at plan time. |

content.text:
Réponse directe à ta question : **il n'y a aucun nom à mettre dans `experiments` sur ce build.** Le fichier source que tu pointes (commit `da87dbb`) enregistre **les 9 expérimentations comme `concluded`**, dont `unknown_instances` — celle qui portait précisément les instances inconnues (`count`/`for_each` à valeur inconnue). Message Terraform : *"Unknown instances are being rolled into a larger feature for deferring unready resources and modules."*

Conséquences concrètes :
- **Retire le bloc `experiments`** : il n'y a plus de keyword actif, donc `experiments = [...]` échoue toujours (`Unknown experiment keyword` ou `Experiment has concluded`). La capacité est désormais **native** dans `1.16.0-alpha20260603`.
- **Pas de flag `-allow-deferral`** dans cet alpha (`plan -help`/`apply -help` ne l'exposent pas) → la déférence est gérée nativement, pas via opt-in CLI. Mon README (et notre hypothèse initiale) sur `-allow-deferral` est donc **à corriger**.

message.content: OK, avance donc sur le plan. Pour rappel, ce que l'on veut faire, c'est très simple. On veut dans le root module des defraudactions, une ressource qui vient créer une clé KMS. On veut un child module qui permet de déployer un bucket S3, qui prend en entrée un string qui va être l'ARN d'une KMS potentiellement existante. et qui va, si cette string est nulle, créer une clé KMS dans le child module pour aller chiffrer le bucket S3. Si cette string est non nulle, elle va simplement utiliser une data source pour la valider, et utiliser cet ARN de manière safe pour le chiffrement du bucket S3. Et nous, ce que l'on veut faire dans le root module, c'est deux instances de ce module. pour tester les deux cas de figure. La première instance va prendre l'ARN résultante de la clé KMS du root module, et la seconde instance ne va rien prendre, et on va bien vérifier que dans les deux cas, ça fonctionne. Voilà, donc propose-moi un plan. Fais attention au nommage des ressources qui doit être précis par rapport à l'usage du module. Le module doit être simple et générique, et l'implémentation du module dans le root module doit être spécifique par rapport au cas d'usage.

content.text:
`ephemeral_values` aussi **concluded** (« can now be used by default, without enabling an experiment ») — donc confirmé : **aucun bloc `experiments`**, capacité native. Et `providers.tf` actuel (sans experiments) valide bien sur l'alpha.

Je trace le plan détaillé dans le todo, puis te le soumets pour go.
